sections.title: Data Structures Overview
sections.content:
**Arrays**: Fixed-size, contiguous memory, O(1) access, O(n) insertion/deletion. Used for storing multiple elements of same type.

**Linked Lists**: Non-contiguous memory, O(n) access, O(1) insertion/deletion if position known. Types: Singly, Doubly, Circular. Advantage: Dynamic size; Disadvantage: Cache unfriendly.

**Stacks (LIFO)**: Last In First Out. Operations: Push O(1), Pop O(1), Peek O(1). Applications: Expression evaluation, Function call stack, Undo/Redo.

**Queues (FIFO)**: First In First Out. Operations: Enqueue O(1), Dequeue O(1). Types: Simple, Circular, Priority, Deque. Applications: Process scheduling, BFS, Message queuing.

**Trees**: Hierarchical structure. Terms: Root, Leaf, Height, Depth. Binary Tree: max 2 children per node. Full Binary Tree: every node has 0 or 2 children. Complete Binary Tree: all levels filled except last. Perfect Binary Tree: all internal nodes have 2 children.

**Binary Search Trees (BST)**: Left < Parent < Right. Search/Insert/Delete: O(log n) average, O(n) worst. Used for fast lookups and maintaining sorted data.

**Hash Tables**: Key-value pairs using hash function. O(1) average access. Collisions handled via Chaining or Open Addressing. Load factor = Elements/Table size. Rehashing when load factor > 0.75.

**Graphs**: Nodes and Edges. Directed vs Undirected. Weighted vs Unweighted. Adjacency Matrix (O(V²) space) vs Adjacency List (O(V+E) space).

**Heaps**: Complete binary tree. Max Heap: Parent ≥ Children. Min Heap: Parent ≤ Children. Operations: Insert O(log n), Delete O(log n), Extract Max/Min O(1). Used in Priority Queues and Heap Sort.

sections.title: Algorithms & Complexity
sections.content:
**Time Complexity**: Measure of execution time. Big O Notation: worst-case scenario. Common complexities: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(n³) < O(2^n) < O(n!).

**Space Complexity**: Memory used by algorithm. Includes auxiliary space (extra space beyond input).

**Asymptotic Notations**: 
- Big O (≤): Upper bound, worst case
- Omega (≥): Lower bound, best case
- Theta (=): Tight bound, average case

**Divide & Conquer**: Break problem into subproblems, solve independently, combine results. Examples: Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search. Recurrence: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). Master Theorem for solving.

**Greedy Algorithms**: Make locally optimal choices hoping to find global optimum. Examples: Activity Selection, Huffman Coding, Dijkstra's, Prim's MST. No guarantee of global optimum but efficient.

**Dynamic Programming**: Optimal substructure + Overlapping subproblems. Memoization (top-down) or Tabulation (bottom-up). Examples: Fibonacci, LCS, 0/1 Knapsack, Matrix Chain Multiplication.

**Backtracking**: Explore all possibilities, abandon path when constraint violated. Examples: N-Queens, Sudoku Solver, Permutations. Time complexity: Exponential.

**NP Completeness**: P problems are solvable in polynomial time. NP problems are verifiable in polynomial time. NP-Complete: in NP and as hard as any NP problem. Examples: SAT, TSP, Knapsack. No known polynomial solution.

sections.title: Sorting & Searching
sections.content:
**Sorting Algorithms**:

**Bubble Sort**: Compare adjacent elements, swap if wrong order. Time: O(n²) worst/avg, O(n) best. Space: O(1). Stable. Used: Small datasets.

**Selection Sort**: Find minimum, place at beginning. Time: O(n²) all cases. Space: O(1). Unstable. No extra comparisons.

**Insertion Sort**: Build sorted array one item at a time. Time: O(n²) worst/avg, O(n) best. Space: O(1). Stable. Good for small/nearly sorted data.

**Merge Sort**: Divide into halves, merge sorted halves. Time: O(n log n) all cases. Space: O(n). Stable. Preferred for linked lists.

**Quick Sort**: Partition using pivot, recursively sort. Time: O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst. Space: O(log n) avg (recursion stack). Unstable. Fastest average case. Pivot selection crucial (median-of-three).

**Heap Sort**: Build max heap, extract elements. Time: O(n log n) all cases. Space: O(1). Unstable. Guarantees O(n log n).

**Counting Sort**: Count occurrences, place in order. Time: O(n+k) where k=range. Space: O(k). Stable. For integers with limited range.

**Radix Sort**: Sort by digits/characters. Time: O(nk) where k=number of digits. Space: O(n+k). Stable. Fast for large numbers.

**Searching Algorithms**:

**Linear Search**: Scan each element. Time: O(n). Used: Unsorted or small arrays.

**Binary Search**: Divide sorted array in half. Time: O(log n). Space: O(1) iterative, O(log n) recursive. Requires sorted array.

**Ternary Search**: Divide into thirds. Time: O(log₃ n). Rarely used, similar to binary search.

sections.title: Operating System Concepts
sections.content:
**Processes vs Threads**: Process is independent program instance with own memory (4GB each typically). Thread is lightweight, shares process memory, faster creation/context switching. Multiple threads in one process enable parallelism within single program.

**CPU Scheduling**: Determines which process runs when. Preemptive: OS can interrupt. Non-preemptive: process runs to completion. Algorithms:
- FCFS (First Come First Served): Simple, Convoy effect
- SJF (Shortest Job First): Minimal average wait time, Starvation
- Round Robin: Fair, Time quantum critical
- Priority Scheduling: Starvation risk
- Multilevel Queue: Different priority classes

**Deadlock**: Circular wait for resources. Conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait. Prevention: Break one condition. Detection: Resource allocation graph. Recovery: Abort process, preempt resource.

**Synchronization**: Critical Section: code accessing shared resource. Mutex (binary semaphore): 0 or 1. Semaphore: counter-based. Race Condition: unpredictable outcome due to concurrent access. Solved by locks, semaphores, monitors.

**Memory Management**: Virtual Memory: program uses logical addresses mapped to physical. Paging: fixed-size blocks. Segmentation: variable-size blocks. Page Replacement: LRU, FIFO, Optimal. Thrashing: excessive paging, reduces performance.

**File System**: Directories organize files hierarchically. Inode (file metadata): permissions, size, pointers. FAT, NTFS, ext4 are file system types. Fragmentation: scattered data reduces speed. Defragmentation restores continuity.

**Shell & Commands**: Command interpreter translating user input to OS actions. Pipes (|), Redirection (>, <), Background (&), Wildcards (*,?). Standard streams: stdin (0), stdout (1), stderr (2).

sections.title: Computer Networks Basics
sections.content:
**OSI Model (7 Layers)**:
1. Physical: Cables, hubs, electrical signals
2. Data Link: MAC addresses, switches, Ethernet, framing
3. Network: IP addresses, routers, routing (RIP, OSPF, BGP)
4. Transport: TCP, UDP, ports, end-to-end delivery
5. Session: Session management, dialog control
6. Presentation: Encryption, compression, data format conversion
7. Application: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH

**TCP/IP Model**: 4 layers - Link, Internet, Transport, Application. Practical implementation.

**IP Addressing**: IPv4 (32-bit, 4 octets: 192.168.1.1). IPv6 (128-bit, 8 groups). Classful: A (0-126), B (128-191), C (192-223). Classless (CIDR): /24 notation. Subnet Mask determines network portion.

**TCP vs UDP**: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol): Reliable, ordered, connection-oriented, slow, used for HTTP, FTP, Email. UDP (User Datagram Protocol): Unreliable, unordered, connectionless, fast, used for DNS, video streaming, gaming.

**DNS**: Domain Name System. Resolves domain names to IP addresses. Hierarchical: Root → TLD (.com, .org) → Authoritative nameservers. Records: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME (alias), MX (mail).

**HTTP vs HTTPS**: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) - port 80, unsecured. HTTPS - port 443, encrypted with TLS/SSL. Status codes: 2xx (success), 3xx (redirect), 4xx (client error), 5xx (server error).

**Routing**: Determines path for packets. Static: manual configuration. Dynamic: RIP (distance vector), OSPF (link state), BGP (exterior gateway).

**Bandwidth & Latency**: Bandwidth: data transfer capacity (bits/sec). Latency: time for data to travel (ms). Throughput: actual data transferred, affected by congestion.

sections.title: DBMS Fundamentals
sections.content:
**Database Models**: Relational (tables with rows/columns, SQL) - most common. NoSQL (document, key-value, graph) - flexible schema. Hierarchical (tree structure, outdated). Network (graph structure).

**ACID Properties**: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (valid state), Isolation (concurrent independence), Durability (persistent). Ensures data reliability.

**Normalization**: Remove redundancy through normal forms. 1NF: Atomic values, no repeating groups. 2NF: 1NF + no partial dependencies. 3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependencies. BCNF: Stricter than 3NF. Denormalization: intentional redundancy for performance.

**Keys**: Primary Key: unique identifier (not null). Foreign Key: links to another table's primary key. Candidate Key: potential primary key. Super Key: unique superset. Surrogate Key: artificial primary key.

**Joins**: INNER JOIN: matching records only. LEFT JOIN: all from left + matching. RIGHT JOIN: all from right + matching. FULL OUTER JOIN: all records. CROSS JOIN: cartesian product.

**Indexes**: Data structure for fast lookup. B-Tree, Hash indexes. Primary index (on key), Secondary index (on other columns). Trade-off: faster queries but slower inserts/updates.

**Transactions**: COMMIT (save changes), ROLLBACK (undo changes), SAVEPOINT (partial rollback). Locking: Shared (read) vs Exclusive (write). Deadlock possible with multiple locks.

**Backup & Recovery**: Full backup (all data), Incremental (changes since last), Differential (changes since full). RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) for fault tolerance.

**SQL Basics**: DDL (CREATE, ALTER, DROP), DML (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), DCL (GRANT, REVOKE), TCL (COMMIT, ROLLBACK). WHERE, ORDER BY, GROUP BY, HAVING for filtering/grouping.

sections.title: Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)
sections.content:
**SDLC Models**:

**Waterfall**: Sequential phases (Requirement → Design → Development → Testing → Deployment). Rigid, documentation-heavy, suitable for fixed requirements. Risk: late testing.

**Iterative**: Multiple cycles of development. Feedback incorporated each iteration. Flexible, continuous improvement. Risk: scope creep.

**Agile**: Short sprints (1-4 weeks), iterative delivery, continuous feedback, adaptive planning. Scrum (roles: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Team) and Kanban (continuous flow). Benefits: Fast delivery, customer collaboration. Challenge: requires discipline.

**DevOps**: Development + Operations integration. Continuous Integration (CI): automated testing, merge frequently. Continuous Deployment (CD): automated release to production. Tools: Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions. Culture of collaboration and automation.

**SDLC Phases**:
1. **Planning**: Define scope, feasibility, resources, timeline
2. **Requirements**: Gather and document functional/non-functional requirements
3. **Design**: Architecture, database schema, UI/UX mockups, technical specifications
4. **Development**: Code implementation following design
5. **Testing**: Unit (individual components), Integration (component interaction), System (full system), UAT (user acceptance). Types: Functional, Performance, Security, Regression
6. **Deployment**: Release to production, user training, documentation
7. **Maintenance**: Bug fixes, updates, performance monitoring, user support

**Version Control**: Git (distributed), SVN (centralized). Commits track changes. Branches for features. Merge conflicts need resolution. GitFlow (structured branching), GitHub Flow (simple).

**Code Quality**: Code reviews, static analysis, testing coverage (aim >80%). Refactoring: improve code without changing behavior. Technical debt: shortcuts creating future problems.

sections.title: Cloud & DevOps Basics
sections.content:
**Cloud Computing Models**:

**IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)**: Virtualized computing resources over internet. Rent servers, storage, networking. Examples: AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud. Customer manages OS, middleware, applications. Provider manages hardware.

**PaaS (Platform as a Service)**: Development platform with tools, databases, middleware. Build and deploy applications. Examples: Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine. Customer manages applications. Provider manages platform.

**SaaS (Software as a Service)**: Fully managed applications. Access via web browser. Examples: Salesforce, Microsoft 365, Google Workspace. No installation needed. Customer just uses. Provider manages everything.

**Deployment Models**: Public (shared resources, cost-effective), Private (dedicated, secure), Hybrid (public + private), Community (shared by organizations).

**Containerization**: Docker: packages application with dependencies in lightweight containers. Benefits: consistency across environments, fast deployment, resource efficiency. Docker image (blueprint) → Container (running instance). Docker Compose for multi-container apps. Kubernetes (K8s) for orchestration: manages containers, scaling, updates, networking.

**CI/CD Pipeline**: Code push → Automated build → Automated tests → Deploy to staging → Manual approval → Deploy to production. Tools: Jenkins (orchestration), GitHub Actions, GitLab CI. Benefits: faster releases, fewer bugs, quick feedback.

**Infrastructure as Code (IaC)**: Manage infrastructure through code/scripts. Tools: Terraform, Ansible, CloudFormation. Benefits: version control, reproducibility, consistency, rapid provisioning.

**Monitoring & Logging**: Log aggregation: ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana). Monitoring tools: Prometheus, Datadog, New Relic. Metrics: CPU, memory, disk, network, request rate, latency, error rate. Alerts for anomalies. APM (Application Performance Monitoring) tracks user experience.

**Microservices**: Small, independent services. Benefits: scalability, flexibility, independent deployment. Challenges: network latency, distributed transactions, debugging complexity.
